subject: Economics
contextNote: JC2 Economics, 24 students. You modelled a positive demand shock — a rise in government spending shifts the AD curve right, creating an inflationary gap where actual output exceeds potential output, and triggering a gradual wage-price adjustment through a leftward SRAS shift until a new long-run equilibrium is reached at a higher price level and the same potential output. Students are now 28 minutes into a set of AD-AS shock questions. Most have drawn the correct final equilibrium — but the adjustment path between the shock and the outcome is invisible in almost every book.

students.name: Rajan
students.note: Correct new equilibrium drawn precisely. Arrows show the AD shift, but the adjustment path is instant — no sticky wages, no SRAS shift, no inflationary gap labelled.

students.name: Nur Atiqah
students.note: Correct final equilibrium marked. The space between the shock and the outcome is completely blank — no adjustment path drawn at all.

students.name: Wei Jie
students.note: Correct outcome, but redraws over partial adjustment paths whenever you approach. Faint pencil marks of SRAS arrows visible under the redrawn lines.

students.name: Siti
students.note: Correct final equilibrium marked. No mechanism notes, no adjustment arrows. Cannot explain how she got there when asked.

ep1.situation: 28 minutes into the lesson. You circulate and the graphs look right — most students have the correct new long-run equilibrium marked. But the adjustment paths are invisible. Rajan has drawn the correct new equilibrium with the AD shift arrow and the new price level and output clearly marked. But his adjustment is instant — the diagram jumps from the shock straight to the new equilibrium with no inflationary gap labelled, no SRAS shift, no mention of rising wages or price pressure. The path between the demand shock and the new equilibrium simply does not exist on his page. Nur has the correct final equilibrium — higher price level, same potential output — but the space between the initial equilibrium and the final one is completely blank. There is no adjustment arrow, no SRAS shift, no intermediate step of any kind. Her diagram shows a before and after, not a mechanism. Wei Jie has the correct outcome, but as you approach his desk, you see him redrawing over something. Under the fresh lines, you can see faint pencil marks — partial SRAS arrows and what looks like an inflationary gap bracket — that he has drawn over and covered. He is erasing his reasoning as you arrive. Siti has the correct final equilibrium marked on her diagram. When you ask her how she got there, she shrugs and looks at the floor. There are no mechanism notes, no adjustment arrows, no intermediate steps of any kind. A quick scan of the class: 21 of 24 students have the correct new equilibrium. But only 9 students have drawn any adjustment path at all, and only 4 have labelled the inflationary gap and the SRAS shift with any explanation. The room looks productive. The books tell a different story. You have about 10 minutes of lesson time remaining.
ep1.evidence: The final equilibria are mostly correct, but the adjustment mechanism — the inflationary gap, the rise in wages as firms compete for labour, the leftward SRAS shift, and the return to potential output at a higher price level — is hidden or absent in almost every book. The gap is not in drawing the AD shift or the final position but in understanding and showing the gradual transmission path between the shock and the new long-run equilibrium.

choices.label: Two-phase path prompt: initial shock, then sticky adjustment
choices.elaboration: Stop the class and project a structured prompt onto the board: 'For your AD shock, answer in two phases. Phase 1 — Initial impact: What happens immediately after the shock? Label the new AD position, the inflationary gap, and the short-run price and output. Phase 2 — Sticky adjustment: What happens over time? Why does SRAS shift, and in which direction? Where does the economy end up in the long run?' Ask students to write both phases in the space below their diagrams. Give 3 minutes. Circulate and look at Phase 2 specifically — this is where the wage-price stickiness mechanism will either appear or remain invisible. After 3 minutes, ask two students to share their Phase 2 explanations.
choices.tradeoff: The two-phase structure separates the immediate impact of the shock from the gradual adjustment mechanism, making the missing step explicit without simply telling students what to write. It gives you whole-class evidence of who understands the transmission path and who is drawing the final equilibrium without understanding how the economy gets there. But the two-phase structure may scaffold too heavily — some students may write the correct steps without understanding why each step happens. Siti may copy the phase labels from the board without being able to explain the mechanism behind them.

choices.label: Pair-trace dynamics: fast students explain the path aloud
choices.elaboration: Identify students who have drawn the adjustment path — even partially — and pair them with students who have only the final equilibrium. Ask the student with the path to trace it aloud using the stem: 'After the AD shift, the economy is at ___ because ___. Then ___ happens because ___. Eventually the economy reaches ___ because ___.' The listening student must ask one clarifying question: 'Why does the adjustment happen gradually rather than instantly?' Circulate for 2 minutes and listen for pairs where the wage-price stickiness mechanism is being articulated versus pairs where students are describing the diagram without the economics. After 2 minutes, ask one strong pair to trace their path for the class.
choices.tradeoff: Pair-trace elicits the adjustment mechanism through low-stakes peer explanation and makes the hidden path visible in conversation before it appears in writing. The 'why gradually?' question targets the wage stickiness concept directly — students have to articulate the mechanism, not just trace the arrows. But the quality of the exchange depends on whether the fluent student can explain the mechanism rather than just describe the diagram. Siti, who cannot explain how she got her answer, may copy her partner's trace without building genuine understanding. Copying risk rises quickly in pairs where the mechanism is only partially understood by either student.

choices.label: IRF (Initiate-Response-Feedback) path chains: whole-class verbal mechanism reconstruction
choices.elaboration: Pause the class and run a whole-class IRF probe focused on the adjustment mechanism. Ask: 'After the government spending shock shifts AD right — what happens next? Why doesn't the economy jump straight to the new long-run equilibrium?' (Initiation). Take the first response, then ask a second student: 'Can you build on that? What is the mechanism that makes the adjustment gradual rather than instant?' (Response). Then ask a third student: 'And what specifically causes SRAS to shift in the long run? What is the chain from the inflationary gap to the final equilibrium?' (Feedback). Use the IRF chain to build a class-constructed explanation of the wage-price stickiness transmission. Write the key steps on the board as they emerge.
choices.tradeoff: The IRF chain makes the adjustment mechanism visible and public through student voices rather than teacher explanation. It builds a shared, class-constructed understanding of the transmission path and creates a culture of mechanism reasoning rather than outcome matching. The chain also gives you real-time evidence of which students can articulate the path and which cannot. But the chain is driven by students who can verbalise their reasoning — students who are drawing correctly without understanding the mechanism, like Rajan, may give the right steps without explaining why. And students who have no mechanism, like Siti, remain passive observers unless deliberately drawn in.

A.consequence: The two-phase responses come in and the pattern is revealing. 19 of 24 students produced a clear Phase 1 — they can describe the immediate impact of the AD shift and identify the inflationary gap. But Phase 2 is where the split appears: only 8 of 24 wrote any explanation of the sticky adjustment mechanism. Of those 8, 10 wrote 'SRAS shifts left' without explaining why — they have the direction of the shift but not the wage mechanism behind it. Nur wrote a Phase 1 that is correct and precise — she can describe the inflationary gap and the short-run price rise. But her Phase 2 is blank: the adjustment path is still invisible. Rajan wrote both phases fluently: 'Phase 1 — AD shifts right, creating an inflationary gap. Output > potential, price level rises in the short run. Phase 2 — The inflationary gap creates labour shortages. Firms compete for workers, driving wages up. Higher wages raise production costs. SRAS shifts left. The economy returns to potential output at a higher price level.' His explanation is complete and precise. Wei Jie wrote Phase 1 correctly but redraws his Phase 2 and then crosses it out — he had started writing the mechanism and then abandoned it. His Phase 2 is a crossed-out line followed by 'SRAS moves left.' Siti wrote 'Phase 1: AD goes right. Phase 2: new equilibrium.' No mechanism at all. The evidence is now precise: the majority gap is in Phase 2 — the gradual wage-price transmission — not in identifying the shock or its direction. 7 minutes remain.

choices.label: Quick board model: sticky wages to SRAS shift to new LRAS equilibrium
choices.elaboration: Address the Phase 2 gap directly with a rapid, focused board model. Draw the AD-AS diagram and trace the adjustment path step by step, fading as you go: 'Step 1: AD shifts right — inflationary gap opens, output exceeds potential. Step 2: Labour shortages emerge — firms compete for workers, wages rise. Step 3: Higher wages raise production costs — SRAS shifts left. Step 4: As SRAS shifts left, output falls back toward potential and the price level rises further. Step 5: The economy settles at the new long-run equilibrium — same potential output, higher price level.' Write the wage mechanism explicitly: 'Wages are sticky on the way down, but they can rise quickly in a labour shortage — that is what drives the SRAS shift.' Ask all students to complete Phase 2 on their diagrams using this chain. Give 2 minutes.
choices.tradeoff: The rapid board model addresses the Phase 2 gap directly with a complete, visual, step-by-step explanation. It is efficient and will reach the students who had Phase 1 correct but Phase 2 blank — including Nur. The fading structure gives students a model to follow without leaving everything on the board permanently. But the reteach treats the class as uniform at the moment of greatest time pressure. Rajan, who already wrote a complete Phase 2, is under-challenged. Siti, who wrote 'new equilibrium' for Phase 2, may copy the board chain without understanding why each step happens.

choices.label: AQS (Affirm-Question-Suggest) path critique: peers question each other's Phase 2
choices.elaboration: Use the Phase 2 evidence to form pairs quickly: students who wrote a complete adjustment mechanism (like Rajan) are paired with students who have a blank or incomplete Phase 2 (like Nur or Wei Jie). Ask the student with the complete Phase 2 to give AQS feedback on their partner's response: Affirm what is correct in Phase 1 ('Your inflationary gap is right — you've identified the short-run impact correctly'), Question the mechanism ('What happens in Phase 2? Why does SRAS shift left — what is the link between the inflationary gap and wages?'), Suggest the missing step ('Think about what happens to wages when output exceeds potential — who is competing for what?'). Circulate and listen for pairs where the mechanism is being genuinely explained versus pairs where students are just reading each other's Phase 2 answers. Give 3 minutes.
choices.tradeoff: AQS peer feedback uses the expertise already in the room and gives Rajan a genuinely demanding task — explaining the wage-price mechanism in AQS terms requires him to think about what the gap is and how to bridge it, not just demonstrate his own understanding. The structure ensures feedback targets Phase 2 specifically. But Wei Jie crossed out his Phase 2 explanation, so his partner has nothing to affirm or question beyond Phase 1 — the AQS exchange may shift from critiquing to explaining from scratch. Siti's Phase 2 is 'new equilibrium' — her partner may not know where to begin the AQS feedback when there is no mechanism to question.

B.consequence: The pairs form and the room shifts from near-silence to low, focused conversation. Rajan traces his path to his partner with remarkable nuance: 'After the AD shift, there is an inflationary gap — actual output is above potential. That creates pressure in the labour market. Firms are competing for workers, so wages start to rise. Higher wages raise production costs for firms, so SRAS shifts left. As SRAS shifts left, output falls back toward potential and the price level rises further. Eventually, SRAS has shifted enough that output is back at potential — but at a higher price level. The whole thing is gradual because wage negotiations take time.' His partner listens and adds labels to their diagram for the first time. Nur's pair talk reveals a more fragmented understanding: her partner says 'AD shifts right, then SRAS shifts left.' Nur draws the SRAS arrow but then asks: 'Why does SRAS shift left?' Her partner pauses and says: 'Because costs go up?' Nur writes 'costs go up' without understanding the mechanism behind it. Wei Jie begins tracing his path: 'There is an inflationary gap... wages rise... SRAS...' He then stops halfway and says: 'I had this but I wasn't sure.' His partner says: 'Keep going — wages rise, then what?' Wei Jie continues: 'SRAS shifts left.' But he cannot explain why wages rise — he has the sequence without the mechanism. Siti echoes Rajan's explanation from across the room without engaging with it directly. You circulate and hear the pattern: 5 or 6 pairs have genuine mechanism reasoning, where the wage-labour shortage link is being articulated. The majority of pairs are tracing the diagram direction without the economics behind each step. The paths are emerging, but the depth is uneven. 8 minutes remain.

choices.label: IRF (Initiate-Response-Feedback) path probe: demand proof of the sticky mechanism
choices.elaboration: Pause the pair talk and run a focused IRF chain. Ask: 'Rajan, from your explanation — why do wages rise after the inflationary gap? What is the specific mechanism?' (Initiation). Then ask a second student: 'Can you build on that? What does wage stickiness mean for the speed of the SRAS shift?' (Response). Then ask a third student: 'And why doesn't the SRAS shift happen instantly — what makes the adjustment gradual?' (Feedback). Build a class-constructed chain that makes the wage-labour shortage link explicit. Write the key steps on the board: 'Inflationary gap → labour shortage → firms compete for workers → wages rise → production costs rise → SRAS shifts left.' After the chain, ask all students to check their own tracing: 'Does your explanation include the wage mechanism — or just the SRAS arrow?'
choices.tradeoff: The IRF chain leverages Rajan's precise explanation and builds a shared understanding of the wage-labour shortage mechanism — the specific link that most pairs were missing. It deepens the reasoning of the pairs who were tracing correctly and gives the class a shared, precise explanation to check their own work against. But the chain is driven by students who can verbalise the mechanism — Wei Jie, who traced the sequence without the mechanism, and Siti, who was echoing rather than engaging, may follow the chain without connecting it to their own understanding. The probe may expose the pairs who were copying the sequence without the economics.

choices.label: Solo path self-check: three-step chain from shock to equilibrium
choices.elaboration: Stop the pair talk and ask all students to work individually. Write on the board: 'In three steps, explain the adjustment from the AD shock to the new long-run equilibrium. Step 1: What is the immediate impact of the AD shift? Step 2: What is the mechanism that drives the gradual adjustment? (Include: inflationary gap, wages, SRAS.) Step 3: What is the new long-run equilibrium, and why is it at a different price level than the original?' Give students 3 minutes to write, then collect the responses. Scan them to see who has internalised the wage-price transmission and who is still dependent on the pair explanation.
choices.tradeoff: The solo self-check tests whether the pair-trace exchange has built genuine understanding or just transferred diagram labels. Students who genuinely engaged with the mechanism during the pair trace — like Rajan's partner — will be able to write all three steps independently. But the move to individual writing removes the peer support for students who were tracing the sequence without the mechanism behind it. Wei Jie, who traced the steps without explaining the wage link, may stall at Step 2. Siti, who was echoing without engaging, will likely leave Step 2 blank. You will have clean evidence — but with only 8 minutes left, there is limited time to respond to what you find.

C.consequence: The IRF chain begins and the room gets louder with a different quality — not chatter but thinking. You ask: 'After the government spending shock shifts AD right — what happens next? Why doesn't the economy jump straight to the new long-run equilibrium?' Rajan responds first: 'There is an inflationary gap — actual output exceeds potential output. But wages are sticky — they don't rise immediately because of wage contracts. So SRAS doesn't shift instantly.' You take his response and ask a second student to build on it. That student adds: 'Over time, the labour shortage drives wages up — firms compete for workers. As wages rise, production costs rise and SRAS starts shifting left.' Nur contributes when called on: 'I agree — the price level goes up.' She has the direction right but gives only the outcome, not the mechanism. Wei Jie raises his hand when asked: 'After the shock... the inflationary gap...' He pauses and looks uncertain. 'Wages rise?' You confirm and ask him to continue, but he cannot complete the chain. Siti repeats Rajan's words almost verbatim when asked: 'There is an inflationary gap and wages are sticky.' She cannot extend the chain independently. A quick tally: 7 pairs are contributing genuine mechanism reasoning through the chain. Nur and Wei Jie can get the direction but not the full mechanism. Siti is repeating rather than reasoning. 12 pairs have partial verbal chains and 9 are giving only outcomes. The IRF chain has made the mechanism partially visible, but the depth is uneven and you still cannot tell who genuinely understands the wage-labour shortage link. 7 minutes remain.

choices.label: Trio AQS (Affirm-Question-Suggest) on shared verbal chains: target the partial explanations
choices.elaboration: Form trios from the pairs who contributed to the IRF chain. Within each trio, one student shares their verbal chain (as they explained it in the IRF or pair talk), and the other two give AQS feedback: Affirm what was correct ('You identified the inflationary gap and the direction of the SRAS shift correctly'), Question what was missing ('Why do wages rise? What is the specific mechanism that links the inflationary gap to the SRAS shift?'), Suggest the missing step ('Think about the labour market — what happens to wages when firms cannot find enough workers?'). After 2 minutes of trio talk, ask one trio to share the strongest explanation they produced together.
choices.tradeoff: Trio AQS feedback applies the same questioning structure to the verbal chains rather than just the diagrams — it pushes students to examine their own explanations with the same precision as their graphs. The structure is targeted: the affirm step confirms what students got right in the IRF chain, the question step surfaces the missing wage-labour shortage link, and the suggest step gives them the specific mechanism to add. But repeaters like Siti may produce a correct-sounding verbal chain in the trio without understanding the mechanics behind it. The trio structure may protect shallow reasoning if two students in the trio share the same gap.

choices.label: Diagrammed path solo: draw and label the full adjustment chain
choices.elaboration: Stop the whole-class chain and ask all students to work individually. Write on the board: 'Draw the complete AD-AS diagram for the government spending increase. Label every step: (1) Initial equilibrium. (2) AD shift and inflationary gap. (3) Wage mechanism — why SRAS shifts. (4) SRAS shift direction and size. (5) New long-run equilibrium — price level and output.' Tell students: 'You have 4 minutes. Your diagram must show the path, not just the start and end. Collect three diagrams — one complete, one partial, one with only the outcome — after the time is up.'
choices.tradeoff: Individual path diagrams give you the clearest evidence of each student's understanding of the transmission mechanism — uncontaminated by peer talk or verbal chain participation. A labelled diagram cannot hide the absence of the wage mechanism the way a verbal exchange can. The five-step labelling requirement means students cannot skip from the shock straight to the new equilibrium without showing the path. But the move to solo drawing kills the emerging talk momentum and gives students who were genuinely building understanding through the IRF chain less time to consolidate. Siti, who was repeating rather than reasoning, will likely stop after Step 2 — the diagram will give you clean evidence of her gap, but no time to address it.

A1.outcome: You draw the AD-AS diagram on the board and trace the five-step adjustment path quickly and clearly. The room goes quiet. Nur watches closely and you see the moment the mechanism clicks — when you say 'firms are competing for workers, so wages rise,' she picks up her pen and writes it down before you move to the next step. She completes Phase 2 on her diagram with the wage mechanism included for the first time: 'Inflationary gap → labour shortage → wages rise → production costs rise → SRAS shifts left → returns to potential output at higher price level.' Wei Jie looks at the board and then looks at his own crossed-out Phase 2. He rewrites it, this time leaving it visible: 'Wages rise because firms compete for workers. SRAS shifts left because costs rise.' He writes slowly, as if checking each step against the board. Rajan copies the board notes compliantly but with obvious disengagement — his body language shows he is waiting for the model to finish. He adds a note in the margin: 'Speed of SRAS shift depends on degree of wage stickiness and length of contracts.' Siti copies the board model carefully, step by step. When you circulate and ask her 'Why do wages rise after the inflationary gap?', she looks at the board and reads: 'Firms compete for workers.' Her answer is correct but board-dependent — she is reading, not reasoning. On the next question, 22 of 24 students now draw the full adjustment path with the wage mechanism labelled, up from 9 at the start. The Phase 2 gap has been largely addressed. But the understanding is now uniform — you cannot tell from the diagrams who genuinely understands the wage-labour shortage link and who has copied the model. Rajan is under-challenged and Siti is dependent on the board. 5 minutes remain.
A1.tradeoff: The rapid board model is efficient and reaches the majority who had Phase 1 correct but Phase 2 blank. Process-level feedback (Hattie & Timperley) addressed the specific adjustment mechanism gap directly. Nur and Wei Jie made clear, visible gains. But the reteach at the point of maximum time pressure treats the class as uniform and homogenises the variety of understanding that the two-phase prompt had just revealed. Rajan is under-challenged and Siti's understanding remains board-dependent — she has copied the chain without being able to reconstruct it independently.
A1.reflection:
Why did you choose a rapid board model at this point in the lesson — with only 7 minutes remaining? What were you prioritising — coverage, clarity, or equity?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? 22 students now draw the full adjustment path, but Rajan is disengaged and Siti is reading from the board rather than reasoning. What does this tell you about the trade-off between efficiency and genuine understanding in an invisible-understanding classroom?

How do you design the Phase 2 task so that students must articulate the wage mechanism before you model it — rather than after?

A2.outcome: You form the pairs quickly and the room shifts to focused conversation. Rajan looks at Nur's Phase 2 — blank — and begins his AQS feedback: 'Your Phase 1 is exactly right — you've identified the inflationary gap and the short-run price rise clearly. But Phase 2 is missing. Let me ask you: what happens to wages when output is above potential?' Nur thinks and says: 'They rise?' Rajan confirms: 'Yes — because firms are competing for workers. Labour is scarce, so wages go up. And when wages go up...' Nur continues: 'Production costs go up. So SRAS shifts left.' She writes this in her Phase 2 as she says it. Rajan's AQS has moved from affirm to question to suggest, and Nur has built the mechanism rather than just received it. Wei Jie is paired with a student who noticed his crossed-out Phase 2. His partner says: 'You had something here — what did you write?' Wei Jie says: 'I wasn't sure about the wages part.' His partner gives AQS: 'Your Phase 1 is solid. For Phase 2 — why do wages rise? What creates the labour pressure?' Wei Jie says: 'The inflationary gap means output is above potential, so firms need more workers than are available.' His partner confirms: 'Yes — that is the mechanism. Write it.' Wei Jie rewrites his Phase 2 with confidence. Siti is paired with a student who can describe the steps but not the economics. Her partner says: 'Phase 2 is SRAS shifts left.' Siti writes this without a mechanism. When you ask her 'Why does SRAS shift left?', she says 'My partner said so.' 5 minutes remain.
A2.tradeoff: AQS peer feedback activated the expertise in the room at the point of greatest time pressure. Rajan deepened Nur's understanding of the wage mechanism through structured questioning rather than explanation — Nur built the chain rather than receiving it. Wei Jie's partner prompted him to articulate the mechanism he had crossed out, and he reconstructed it with confidence. But Siti's partner could not model the wage mechanism and gave only the SRAS direction — Siti has a slightly better Phase 2 but still no understanding of why SRAS shifts.
A2.reflection:
Why did you choose peer AQS feedback over a quick board model with only 7 minutes left? What were you prioritising?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Nur and Wei Jie made genuine gains through structured peer questioning, but Siti's partner gave her the answer without the mechanism. What does this tell you about how pair quality affects the equity of AQS feedback at the end of a lesson?

How do you ensure that AQS pairs are formed so that the student giving feedback can model the missing mechanism — not just supply the missing step?

B1.outcome: You pause the pair talk and run the IRF probe. You ask: 'Rajan, from your explanation — why do wages rise after the inflationary gap? What is the specific mechanism?' Rajan responds: 'The inflationary gap means actual output is above potential. That means firms need more workers than the labour market can supply at current wages. There is a labour shortage. Firms compete for workers by offering higher wages.' You ask a second student to build on this: 'And wage stickiness — why do wages rise gradually rather than instantly?' That student responds: 'Because of wage contracts. Wages are agreed in advance, so they cannot rise immediately. But over time, as contracts expire and are renegotiated, wages do rise.' A third student connects to SRAS: 'As wages rise, production costs rise. So SRAS shifts left. The higher costs reduce the incentive to produce at the previous output level.' The IRF chain produces a rich, class-built explanation of the wage-labour shortage transmission. You write the key steps on the board: 'Inflationary gap → labour shortage → firms compete for workers → wages rise (gradually, due to contracts) → production costs rise → SRAS shifts left → economy returns to potential output at higher price level.' Nur listens and immediately adds the wage mechanism to her path trace. Wei Jie follows and adds the labour shortage step he was missing. Rajan listens with engagement — the chain has articulated the mechanism more precisely than his own explanation, and he writes 'contract renegotiation lag' in his notes. Siti watches but does not contribute. When you ask 'Does your path show the wage mechanism?', she nods without writing anything new. 5 minutes remain.
B1.tradeoff: The IRF chain leveraged Rajan's explanation and built a precise, class-constructed account of the wage-labour shortage transmission mechanism — richer than any individual student's explanation alone. The chain deepened understanding for Nur and Wei Jie, and the precision of the class-built language extended even Rajan's thinking. But the chain is driven by students who can articulate the mechanism publicly — Siti's surface-level understanding is not surfaced or addressed, and she leaves the IRF sequence without any new reasoning on her page.
B1.reflection:
Why did you choose to run an IRF chain after the pair-trace phase rather than moving directly to individual application? What were you hoping the class would learn?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? The chain produced precise mechanism reasoning and extended even Rajan, but Siti's understanding remains unchanged. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for whole-class IRF probes to include students who are echoing rather than reasoning?

How do you design the pair-trace and IRF sequence so that students like Siti — who can echo but not extend — are drawn into genuine mechanism reasoning rather than passive listening?

B2.outcome: You write the new scenario on the board — 'A rise in government spending increases aggregate demand' — and ask students to write the three-step chain from shock to equilibrium independently. Rajan writes a complete chain immediately: 'Step 1: AD shifts right, inflationary gap opens — actual output exceeds potential. Price level rises in the short run. Step 2: Labour shortage emerges. Firms compete for workers — wages rise gradually as contracts expire and are renegotiated. Higher wages raise production costs. SRAS shifts left. Step 3: SRAS continues shifting until output returns to potential at a higher price level. New long-run equilibrium: same Y-potential, higher P.' His self-check adds: 'The adjustment is gradual because wage contracts mean wages cannot rise instantly — the SRAS shift takes time.' His chain is complete and precise. Nur writes Steps 1 and 2 correctly — she has internalised the wage mechanism from the pair trace: 'Inflationary gap → labour shortage → wages rise → SRAS shifts left.' Her Step 3 is brief but accurate. Wei Jie writes Step 1 correctly and begins Step 2: 'Labour shortage → wages...' He cannot complete it in writing — he writes 'wages rise because of shortage' and then stops. His Step 3 is correct but arrived at without the full mechanism. Siti writes Step 1 and leaves Steps 2 and 3 blank. Her self-check says: 'I explained this with my partner.' The solo chain has given you the clearest picture of the lesson: Rajan and Nur have genuine understanding of the full wage-price transmission; Wei Jie has the sequence without the economics; Siti has the shock and nothing beyond it. 5 minutes remain.
B2.tradeoff: The solo self-check tests whether the pair-trace exchange has built genuine understanding or just transferred diagram labels. Rajan and Nur demonstrated genuine internalisation of the wage mechanism — they could write the full transmission chain independently. But Wei Jie has the sequence without the causal link, and Siti cannot work without her partner. The move to solo writing reveals that the pair trace built understanding unevenly — genuine transfer for some students and surface sequence for others.
B2.reflection:
Why did you move from pair-trace to individual self-check? What were you trying to find out?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan and Nur showed genuine understanding, but Wei Jie has the sequence without the mechanism and Siti cannot work independently. What does this tell you about the difference between tracing a path with a partner and genuinely internalising the economic mechanism behind it?

How do you design the transition from pair-trace to individual application so that students who traced the path without understanding the mechanism have a chance to build the economics — not just reproduce the sequence?

C1.outcome: The trios form and the talk becomes more structured. Rajan shares his IRF contribution — 'inflationary gap, labour shortage, wages rise, SRAS shifts left' — and his two partners give AQS feedback. One affirms: 'You've got the full chain — inflationary gap to labour market to SRAS is exactly right.' The second questions: 'But why do wages rise gradually? You said it happens over time — what is the mechanism for the delay?' Rajan engages immediately: 'Wage contracts. Workers and firms have agreed wages in advance. Until those contracts expire and are renegotiated, wages cannot rise — so the SRAS shift is slow.' He writes 'contract renegotiation lag' as a note. Nur's trio gives AQS on her partial chain. Her partner questions: 'You said 'costs go up' for the SRAS shift — what specifically causes costs to go up?' Nur says: 'Wages — firms have to pay more for workers.' She adds the wage link to her explanation. Wei Jie's trio affirms his direction but questions his mechanism: 'You said 'wages rise because of shortage' — why does the shortage cause wages to rise? Who is doing what?' Wei Jie thinks and says: 'Firms are competing for workers, so they offer higher wages to attract them.' His partner confirms. Siti shares her IRF contribution — 'there is an inflationary gap and wages are sticky' — and her partners give AQS. One asks: 'Why are wages sticky? What does sticky mean?' Siti says: 'I heard Rajan say it.' She cannot explain the concept. Her trio's AQS reveals that she has been repeating without understanding. 4 minutes remain.
C1.tradeoff: Trio AQS on verbal chains applied structured feedback to the IRF explanations and pushed students to examine the mechanism behind their own words. Rajan was extended by his partners' question about the delay mechanism — a subtlety he had not articulated. Nur and Wei Jie each added a missing causal link through the questioning. But Siti's trio AQS exposed that she has been repeating without understanding — 'I heard Rajan say it' is the clearest evidence yet that the verbal chain has not built genuine understanding for her.
C1.reflection:
Why did you choose trio AQS on verbal chains rather than asking students to draw the full path diagram individually? What were you prioritising?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan was extended, Nur and Wei Jie made genuine gains, and Siti's gap was finally made visible — but there are only 4 minutes left to address it. What does this tell you about the timing of AQS feedback in a lesson where the mechanism gap appears late?

How do you design trio AQS so that students who are repeating rather than reasoning — like Siti — are identified earlier in the lesson, when there is still time to address the conceptual gap?

C2.outcome: The room goes quiet as students draw their individual path diagrams. Rajan produces a complete five-step diagram with every label in place — initial equilibrium, AD shift with inflationary gap bracket, wage mechanism note ('labour shortage → wage contracts expire → wages rise → costs rise'), SRAS shift with direction and distance, and new long-run equilibrium with price level and output clearly labelled. His diagram is precise and complete. Nur draws Steps 1 through 4 correctly. Her wage mechanism note says 'firms compete for workers → wages rise → costs rise → SRAS shifts left.' Her Step 5 is correct. Her diagram shows genuine understanding of the full transmission path. Wei Jie draws Steps 1 and 2 correctly and begins Step 3 — he draws the SRAS shift in the correct direction but labels it 'costs rise' without the wage mechanism. His Step 4 is correct. He cannot label Step 3 with the labour shortage and wage link — the mechanism is still invisible in his diagram. Siti draws Steps 1 and 2 and then stops. Her diagram shows the AD shift and the inflationary gap bracket but nothing beyond it. When you ask 'What comes after the inflationary gap?', she says: 'Wages rise?' You ask: 'Why?' She shakes her head. The solo diagrams have given you the clearest, most honest picture of the lesson: Rajan and Nur have the full transmission path; Wei Jie has the structure without the specific wage mechanism; Siti has the shock and nothing beyond. The IRF chain was driven by the students who already understood — it deepened their reasoning but did not build the mechanism for students who were echoing. 4 minutes remain.
C2.tradeoff: Individual path diagrams give you the most honest evidence of each student's understanding — a labelled diagram cannot hide the absence of the wage mechanism the way verbal participation can. Rajan and Nur demonstrated genuine understanding of the full transmission. But the solo diagram revealed that the IRF chain built understanding for the students who contributed — and left Wei Jie and Siti with incomplete or absent mechanisms. The IRF chain was excellent assessment of the students who already knew; it was insufficient to build understanding for the students who were echoing.
C2.reflection:
Why did you move from the IRF chain to individual path diagrams? What were you trying to find out?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan and Nur showed genuine understanding, but Wei Jie has the structure without the specific mechanism and Siti cannot draw beyond the shock. What does this tell you about the limits of whole-class IRF chains as a teaching tool — not just an assessment tool — in an invisible-understanding classroom?

How do you design a lesson so that the wage-price stickiness mechanism becomes visible through student activity — not just through teacher-led chains and board models?
